title: Berlin News
rights: public domain (presumed; pre-1963 broadcast, no rights statement on archive.org item)
transcript: The weather report for today says, today the weather is fine with an atmosphere of spring in Berlin and the suburban districts, although there is a fishwind from the east. Three more incidents are referred to on the German frontiers. On Saturday, 10 tech soldiers attempted to blow up the wooden bridge across the Rev. Taya, in the Beiharth style, which is on German territory. The tech soldiers placed 10 kilos of dynamite under the bridge, and were about to set fire to the railing when they were sighted by two German frontier gods. As the Germans approached, the Czech soldiers fled across the frontier. The dynamite has not yet been removed from underneath the bridge. At nine this morning a Czech military plane flew over the German Czech frontier at bear on stand in the Yetsky Burger. The machine was sighted by a number of people and recognized beyond doubt as a military plane. After the plane which was a two-seater fighter, it circled round for some time, they returned to the frontier and shaped its course towards the town of press nets in Czechoslovakia. On its way back to press nets, the plane suddenly caught fire and crashed on Czech territory. People on this side of the frontier observed how Czech frontier guards and soldiers attempted in vain to rescue the pilots from the blazing debris. Fullermore, check soldiers made on slots on two bridges, Oloy Speck and Hoenfeldt, North of Lins. These bridges are half German, half Czech, and the Czech soldiers add Wunstraw around the wooden supports of the German part of the bridges, and poor petrol over it, but before they were able to set the straw on fire, German frontier guards approached and the Czech soldiers proceed to retreat so into Czech territory. Regarding the incident at the Taya Bridge, the Czech press agency reports that Czech soldiers had actually attempted to enter German territory, but that they had not intended to blow up the bridge. The Czech military authorities reported the incident to the Prague Foreign Office. They repumbed out the crock tar, the Czech foreign minister pleaded an excuse to the German ambassador to Prague. Dealing with an additional version of the Czech news agency report regarding the Taya Bridge incident, the Berliner Tagablet writes that even if the Czech report, according to which Czech soldiers exercised near the bridge, true, the conduct of the military command of that platoon was irresponsible in view of the seriousness of the political situation and cannot be termed otherwise but provocation. The bill in paper further emphasizes this tracking contrast between the tech soldiers and the Sudeten Germans with always shown an exemplary dipsy plan. The result of the municipal elections in Czechoslovakia and the victory of the Sudeten German Party has made a deep impression on all the powers interested in Czechoslovak affairs. The British and French papers again asked Prague to settle the Sudeten German problem according to the demands of Conrad Heinlein. Daily mail declares that the critical situation after the overwhelming victory of the The inline party will force the checks to make conditions. The impregs started in Paris, Nell Square, sustained mail by a way of prompting the check government to act on the spur of the moment, were calculated to cause the explosion which was generally feared. Britain must stand aloof in the matter, whatever happens, and avoid to be drawn into difficulties by pacts with France, the Soviet Union, or Czechoslovakia, or even the Geneva League. It is also being tried in Paris to get out of an unpleasant situation. Petit Paris, here, impressed by the election victory of Conrad Haidlán, is an opinion that Prague might contribute to the general appeasement while sending the reservists home where it so recently been called to the colors. The Paris papers as well as the Lenin press advocate, the realisation of the Sudeten German demands by the Prague government, which could eliminate all difficulties likely to lead to a more serious conflict. According to a report from the press office of the Sudeten German party, the informative discussions between Hakanard Hinlein and Dr. Arger, the Czech Premier, which will serve to clarify and pacify the political situation have begun today. The rapidly melting snow and the rain falls of the last few days have caused floods in Steyermark and in some parts of Counten. Unfortunately, eight persons have lost their lives in the floods. The region leader, Birko, has asked for the relief train bayon, which left Litzig for the distressed area today. The Romanian Premier Miron Cristéa, coming from Warsaw, arrived at Krakow today, where he placed a wreath on the tomb of Marshall Pilsowski. It is stated in political circles of Warsaw that Premier Cristéa, as discussed various These questions connected with the Polish Romanian agreement during her stay in Warsaw. In connection with the rumours of alleged arms transports in South Africa and the speculations on pending disturbances in South West Africa, Deputy Laudanard Burke stated in the Sunday times of Johannesburg that all their apprehension in that respect was unfounded. The administration of South West Africa ascertained with the satisfaction that the room is concerning secret arms impulse that not correspond to the facts. The London afternoon priests also regarded the situation in Tekaslovakia as less critical the days ago. To a certain extent, the London Stakiks changed reactor-hiver for the opposite, but as investments as well as foreign bonds dropped, and the turn of the verb was rather low. In contrast to the rather optimistic view of British and French papers, in connection with a Czech problem, the Budapest government paper exec asks, whether after the events of the last couple of days, it was possible to believe that certain Czech circles actually wanted peace and order. An Evangelion paper to put forth to know that a panic-like atmosphere prevailed in Prague as the nationality's problem had entered into its decisive stage. And responsible people knew quite well that this satisfactory solution of the problem might lead to a European conflict. The last Sonyo's paper, Correa Giovanni, dealing with the situation in Czechoslovakia, says that the Czechs had completely lost their heads, and by their conduct, they rendered the situation still more critical. The nationalities' problem in Czechoslovakia urgently demanded a solution, emphasizes the semi-official Polish paper. According to Polish Newspaper reports, primarily Tvinoff, the Soviet Russian foreign chemistry, traveled from Paris to Prague with a full passport issued for certain Markovsky. Conradly Tvinoff is stated to have had discussions with Dr. Vinish, the Czech president, and Dr. Hodja, the Czech prime minister. The servals who date in German officials in Czechoslovakia were subjected to disciplinary punishment, because they had taken part in the man-making of the sedate in German party. Lord Plenith, speaking in the House of Lords of the Anglo-Maxican or Lispute, said that Britain would only be satisfied by the Mexican government, returning the British-owned oil fails to their legal proprietors. The Paris paper Liberté, dealing with French arms deliveries to Red Spain, declared that since December 26th of last year, every month two ships laid in with arms in ammunition had arrived at Bordeaux, and from there the war material was conveyed to Red Spain by rail and emblauries. The postmortem examination of her Bermond, her off-monger, the two serrated German farmers who were shot dead in anger a couple of days ago proved that they had been fired at from behind and at a distance of only seven yards. Witnesses stated that when the police got fired, the fatal shock Bermond and off-monger motorbike had practically stopped. Thus, the post-mortem examination contradicts the official check report according to which the policeman had aimed at the footwheel, found at the front wheel of the motorbicycle. The interest coupons of the American young on June 1, 1938, which pair the stamp United States of America domicile October 1, 1935 will be purchased in the same way as were the coupons, June 1, 1937. Olders of such coupons will on-due date be able to sell their coupons of their value of $20 for $27.50 nominal at Mrs. J. P. Morgan and company New York City or at one of the American agencies of the Hamburg-American line. On the other hand, young marks can be purchased according to regulations.
